name: Tlalocihuatl
description: Tlalocihuatl, also known as Chalchiuhtlicue, is a prominent Aztec deity revered as the goddess of fresh water, lakes, rivers, springs, and the sea. Her name translates to "She of the Jade Skirt," reflecting her association with preciousness, fertility, and the life-giving properties of water. She is the wife or consort of Tlaloc, the rain god, and together they preside over the watery realms, ensuring the abundance of crops and the continuation of life. Tlalocihuatl is often depicted wearing a jade skirt and a headdress adorned with feathers, symbols of her power and status. She is also associated with childbirth and is considered a protector of pregnant women and newborns. Her powers extend to controlling floods, storms, and the tides, making her both a benevolent provider and a force to be reckoned with. She is a crucial figure in the Aztec pantheon, embodying the vital role of water in sustaining life and ensuring the prosperity of the Aztec civilization. She is often depicted with a child, representing the life that water nourishes. Offerings to Tlalocihuatl often included jade, shells, and other precious materials, reflecting the value placed on water in their society. She is also linked to the concept of purification and cleansing, both physically and spiritually. Some myths depict her as having been replaced by another goddess, showcasing the evolving nature of religious beliefs within Aztec society.
subtitle: Goddess of Fresh Water and Fertility

keyMyths.title: The Jade Skirt
keyMyths.description: Tlalocihuatl's jade skirt symbolizes her power over precious water resources and her role in ensuring fertility. The jade represents the life-giving properties of water.

keyMyths.title: Goddess of Childbirth
keyMyths.description: She is invoked by pregnant women and midwives to ensure a safe delivery and the well-being of the newborn child. Her association with water links her to the amniotic fluid and the beginning of life.

keyMyths.title: The Great Flood
keyMyths.description: Some stories attribute a great flood to Tlalocihuatl's anger or sadness, highlighting her power to both give and take away life through water. This flood marked the end of a previous era.

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de
sources.description: An extensive account of Aztec life and beliefs, including descriptions of Tlalocihuatl.
